# Gke Reliability Performance FAQ ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? What's the process today when there are patches for critical fixes? Do we also change the default version? Our team was interested in seeing if patched versions could have priority over the default versions but we weren't sure if that's already in place in the current process. **Answer:** > how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? We use GKE rollout server to manage the auto upgrade for both cluster kinds. The RS will match cluster with a bunch of gke rollout policies and upgrade the eligible clusters to the desired state given by the policies. google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb > Specifically, how auto-upgrades for patches within minor versions are chosen and upgraded to? We have master patch policies. > Do we also change the default version? Yes if necessary. Eve will generate a list of versions weekly at each rollout cut. The version selection algorithm will pick the best versions to be default or upgrade target. And we will use this version to generate rollout policies. > if patched versions could have priority over the default versions I am not sure I get your idea. But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## GKE auto-upgrade process for standard and autopilot clusters when there are critical patches **Question:** As part of security bulletin notifier and surfacing work our team is doing, could someone on the GKE upgrades team give greater detail on how GKE auto-upgrade versions are determined for both standard and auto-pilot clusters? But there is no priority between versions. Under which condition, you want such behavior? --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** You can also use maintenance windows or exclusions to receive updates at desirable times. --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. 3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** You can also use maintenance windows or exclusions to receive updates at desirable times. --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? 3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** You can also use maintenance windows or exclusions to receive updates at desirable times. --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? 3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** You can also use maintenance windows or exclusions to receive updates at desirable times. --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** You can also use maintenance windows or exclusions to receive updates at desirable times. --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool

4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? 3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. 4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? 3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. 4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? 3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. The cluster is currently configured for auto upgrades and upgrade operations result in heavy disruptions to our workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool
2) Cordon the old nodepool
3) Wait for x amount of time before triggering evictions so that pods in old nodepool run to completion
4) Drain and delete old nodepool Does the GKE Upgrades team have any recommendations on an upgrade configuration to achieve this? Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## Minimizing Disruption due to Upgrades **Question:** Hello, GDCV Baremetal team is currently using a GKE cluster as a prow build cluster for scheduling CI workloads. Since vast majority of the workloads are single invocation pods, the upgrades result in these workloads being evicted prematurely. An ideal upgrade flow in our case would be to 1) Create new nodepool

Thanks. **Answer:** you can use autoscale blue green upgrade, its private preview only feature however and requires CA enabled. But the upgrade flow is as you described, workloads are allowed to run upto 14 days --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.

You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications. While in "running state" the resources are more than enough, when upgrades happen, 20+ applications on one node are rescheduled all at the same time to a new node.
With Spring being resource-hungry at startup, apps throttle, they cant start in time, readiness/liveness start failing and they get restart of pods constantly until 1 by 1 they start, but this takes time. Is there any setting on tip to mitigate this? **Answer:** Hi dariobanfi@
How come 20+ applications are being reassigned to 1 node out of 4? I'm guessing they are using `Surge` Upgrade strategy which (by default) creates only 1 extra node at a time? Please take a look here. Instead of `maxSurge=1` (default), they can use `maxSurge=2 or 3`. If they still see the same issue then, increase this parameter again.. 4 or 5. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#optimizing-surge Alternative solution can be the `blue/green` upgrade strategy. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-pool-upgrade-strategies#blue-green-upgrade-strategy In `Blue/Green` upgrades, GKE creates equal number of extra nodes (~ 4 nodes in this case). cordon and drain the old nodes and reassigns applications to these new 4 nodes. You have much more control and flexibility with `Blue/Green` as compared to `Surge`. Hope it helps! --- ## GKE upgrade process causes performance issues for customer **Question:** Hello, A question of the customer: They have a GKE cluster with 4 nodes, running a large amount of Java Spring applications.
